items.q: Why is the electric field between parallel charged plates considered uniform?
items.correct: Field strength is constant in magnitude and direction throughout the region (away from edges), unlike the inverse-square field of a point charge.
items.misconception: The field still decreases with the inverse square of distance — uniform just means it's smooth.
items.random_wrong: Because the plates are uniform in shape.
items.why: Students extrapolate inverse-square thinking everywhere; they don't distinguish point sources from extended geometries.

items.q: In which direction does an electric field point near a positive charge?
items.correct: Radially outwards — the field is defined by the force on a positive test charge, which is repelled.
items.misconception: Radially inwards because positive attracts negative.
items.random_wrong: There is no field around a positive charge unless another charge is present.
items.why: Students confuse the convention (positive test charge) and forget that an isolated positive source still has a field even without another charge.

items.q: What does gravitational field strength at a point represent?
items.correct: The force per unit mass that would act on a test mass placed at that point.
items.misconception: The total gravitational force at that point regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: The mass of the object creating the field.
items.why: Students forget the 'per unit mass' (or per unit charge) part of field strength definitions, conflating the field with the resulting force.

items.q: Adding a dielectric to a parallel-plate capacitor:
items.why: Correct answer: Increases C. “Decreases C” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Capacitor stores 60 µC at 12 V. Capacitance (µF)?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
